item.title: The вҖҳPeace PresidentвҖҷ Threatens an Apocalypse on Iran. AGAIN
item.description: On this day in 1979, President Jimmy Carter was photographed fending off an angry rabbit while fishing from a boat on a Georgia pond. The story was widely seen to have contributed to CarterвҖҷs defeat by Ronald Reagan in the 1980 election. Hello, readers вҖ“ itвҖҷs Swin again. Some of you have no doubtвҖҰ
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1979, President Jimmy Carter was photographed fending off an angry rabbit while fishing from a boat on a Georgia pond. The story was widely seen to have contributed to CarterвҖҷs defeat by Ronald Reagan in the 1980 election.

Hello, readers вҖ“ itвҖҷs Swin again. Some of you have no doubt grown accustomed to my incessant вҖҳSimpsonsвҖҷ references in this newsletter, seeing as how quoting clips from that show is one of the few ways I know how to adequately convey the sheer stupidity of the Trump-Vance era. This morning, I have another for you: President Donald TrumpвҖҷs negotiation skills during this phase of his illegal war with Iran have been on par with the pre-hockey game deal Chief Wiggum strikes with the criminals at SpringfieldвҖҷs jail. But as this past weekend showed, on top of the self-defeating tactics and bumbling own goals, you also get this presidentвҖҷs mentally desiccated eruptions of insatiable bloodlust.

In todayвҖҷs вҖҳFirst Draft,вҖҷ Trump issues more genocidal threats while oil prices rise and his approval rating craters, and, in Michigan, a centrist Democrat backed by AIPAC and eyeing a seat in the US Senate finds support in short supply.

Nearly two weeks ago, Mehdi and I were on a Zeteo livestream, offering our live reactions to Donald TrumpвҖҷs just announced decision to back off from his own deadline for his own war, for which the self-professed вҖңPEACE PRESIDENTвҖқ had vowed an explicitly genocidal, вҖңcivilizationвҖқ-destroying blitz of war crimes on the Iranian people, if they didnвҖҷt give what he demanded. Trump, thankfully, blinked.

A hyper-tenuous two-week ceasefire (if you can call it that while keeping a straight face) would emerge. Still, on that livestream, I issued a warning to our viewers: That same night, Trump administration officials, citing recent conversations with the president, were stressing to us in real time that the chances that Trump would escalate the war anyways were still extremely high. At the end of the stream, Mehdi asked the panel if weвҖҷd be back in two weeks, talking about how everything went to shit. I wagered less than two weeks, hoping IвҖҷd be wrong.

ItвҖҷs been less than two weeks, and weвҖҷre coming off of a weekend where the former host of NBCвҖҷs вҖҳCelebrity ApprenticeвҖҷ somehow turned president of the United States is not only back to his schtick of casually threatening an apocalyptic barrage of infrastructure-destroying attacks and mass murder, but heвҖҷs once again sharply escalating his war of aggression in the Middle East.

вҖңMy Representatives are going to Islamabad, Pakistan вҖ“ They will be there tomorrow evening, for Negotiations вҖҰ WeвҖҷre offering a very fair and reasonable DEAL, and I hope they take it because, if they donвҖҷt, the United States is going to knock out every single Power Plant, and every single Bridge, in Iran. NO MORE MR. NICE GUY,вҖқ Trump posted Sunday morning.

Later, he announced that US armed forces had, in yet another dramatic escalation, attacked and seized an Iranian-flagged cargo ship near the Strait of Hormuz, as the Americans and Iranians continued their dueling blockades. IranвҖҷs government has effectively shut down the Strait of Hormuz since the start of the war, choking off the flow of oil and sending energy prices soaring, throttling the global economy.

Two senior US officials and two Trump advisers I spoke to this weekend all expected the war to significantly intensify very soon.

As he continues threatening textbook examples of blatant war crimes, Trump is at least publicly claiming that heвҖҷs optimistic about the latest round of supposed peace talks in Pakistan. Unsurprisingly, the Iranians signaled this week that they very much are not and that they consider these peace talks to be a farce. An Iranian official said earlier today that while no final decision had been made, Tehran so far had no plans to attend any upcoming talks in Islamabad.

At this time, all sides of this conflict look like theyвҖҷre slamming clenched fists on the Escalation Button вҖ“ with President Trump inviting even more economic destruction and political suicide. The world desperately needs the Strait of Hormuz open; in recent days, the price of oil shot down on the global market (though not at the US pump yet) because people were expecting (hoping?) this war would wrap up soon. Within the coming days, oil prices will likely spike again, along with the killing, and the Iranian regime is once again threatening to impose greater pain should Trump not officially cry uncle.

Technically, Trump had a way out of thisвҖҰ instead, the war could keep spiraling out of control, in an election year, to an extent where $4 gas might not seem that bad!

Whatever happens in the near term, we as a nation are at a juncture in modern history where it is commonplace, if not wholly expected, for our genocidal president вҖ“ in our name, funded by our hard-earned tax money вҖ“ to dangle horrific, вҖңcivilizationвҖқ-ending war crimes on civilian targets any time he feels like itвҖҰ and for our GOP-controlled Congress to just let him do it whenever he wants.

This is a type of uniquely pathetic barbarism that we as Americans want to believe we only read about, in chapters of world history like the Albigensian Crusade. And yet itвҖҷs routinely coming from the words and actions of our so-called leader of the free world.

We havenвҖҷt even begun to feel the long-term impacts of what Trump and his enablers and allies have done. We will continue reporting on this aggressively here at Zeteo and especially вҖҳFirst DraftвҖҷ in the coming days and weeks. Things are relentlessly bleak, but, heyвҖҰ at least we donвҖҷt have to worry about Donald Trump invading Cuba soon, right?

Death tolls:

Iran: An Iranian official said today that the death toll from US-Israeli strikes is at 3,375, including 383 children. It was unclear whether the death toll also included military and security forces.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,294 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the countryвҖҷs Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 34 people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war newsвҖҰ

Ship seized: The US said it hit and seized an Iranian cargo ship as part of its blockade of Iranian ports, prompting Tehran to promise retaliation, two days shy of the scheduled end of the ceasefire.

Trump gloats: In a post, the US president said the ship had been sanctioned since 2019 and added that US Marines were вҖңseeing whatвҖҷs on board.вҖқ

Barreling on: Oil prices rose, heading back towards $100 a barrel, after news of renewed tensions over the Strait of Hormuz.

Talks fear: European countries worry that US negotiators only care about a quick win for Trump, Reuters reported, adding that diplomats think the American rush to secure a deal with Iran will only guarantee years of complex talks to come.

Christ statue smashed: The Israeli military said a picture of one of its soldiers destroying a statue of Christ in southern Lebanon was real, adding that it вҖңviews the incident with great severityвҖқ and will take вҖңappropriate measuresвҖқ against those involved.

Donald Trump has reached new depths of unpopularity, according to an NBC News poll, with just 37% of Americans approving of his second-term performance and 50% of those who disapproved saying they did so strongly. A full two-thirds of respondents said they disapproved of TrumpвҖҷs handling of inflation and the conflict with Iran. One area where those two issues meet is at the gas pump: Two-thirds of Americans answering the survey said gas prices were a problem for them and their family. Trump was even down four points with Republicans, though 83% still backed him.

According to Politico, GOP unease is spreading to operatives in battleground states, who are beginning to voice real concern that TrumpвҖҷs unpopularity could see the Senate, and not just the House, fall to the Democrats in November.

Too true: US Ambassador to the UN Mike Waltz made an apparent Freudian slip, referring to TrumpвҖҷs attack on Iran, Operation Epic Fury, as вҖңOperation Economic Fury.вҖқ

Boos news: Michigan US Senate hopeful and AIPAC favorite Representative Haley Stevens was loudly booed as she spoke at the state Democratic convention.

Screens test: The Strokes ended their set at Coachella by displaying protest messages highlighting US-backed coups, killings, and interventions, including the war in Iran and support for IsraelвҖҷs attacks on Gaza.

Kash dash: FBI director Kash Patel said yesterday that a lawsuit against The Atlantic over its report about his alleged excessive drinking and unexplained absences was вҖңcoming tomorrow.вҖқ The Atlantic editor Jeffrey Goldberg said he stood by the story.

ICE watch: A federal judge in Long Island described вҖңpolice stateвҖқ tactics employed by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), including arresting people and drawing up warrants after the fact.

Court scoop: The New York Times published secret memos that showed how the Supreme Court in 2016 created the вҖңshadow docket,вҖқ the controversial mechanism that has created rulings in favor of the Trump administration.

Another progressive wins: Defeating the incumbent, Amir Makled, a lawyer who represented University of Michigan student protesters, won the Democratic nomination for the schoolвҖҷs governing board during the partyвҖҷs state endorsement convention this weekend.

Which Premier League football team does FBI director Kash Patel appear to support, judging by his controversial neckwear choices for visits to Congress?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

Is the US violating the вҖҳceasefireвҖҷ with Iran?

Join Mehdi and Prem TODAY at 11am ET, 8am PT, or 4pm GMT, for ZeteoвҖҷs вҖҳAsk the Editor.вҖҷ

рҹҮөрҹҮё DonвҖҷt forget Gaza: Hamas officials say theyвҖҷre willing to turn over some weapons to the Palestinian administrative committee, according to the New York Times. Meanwhile, Haaretz reported that Palestinians in Gaza say theyвҖҷre having to stand in increasingly long lines to wait for bread as flour and fuel shortages worsen amid IsraelвҖҷs continued restrictions on aid entering the Strip.

рҹҮ¬рҹҮ§ рҹҮөрҹҮё Spying on students: Al Jazeera reported today that 12 universities in the UK paid hundreds of thousands of dollars to a private firm to вҖңspyвҖқ on student protesters and academics, including Palestine supporters and animal rights activists.

рҹҮ§рҹҮ¬ Electoral shift: The center-left Progressive Bulgaria party appeared to win BulgariaвҖҷs election, according to exit polls. It beat the center-right GERB party, which has long dominated, but a coalition government will need to be formed.

рҹҮЁрҹҮ© Temporary truce: The Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and the M23 rebel group took first steps towards a long-term peace deal, agreeing to allow aid deliveries, release prisoners, and stop targeting civilians.

рҹҮІрҹҮҫ Devastating fire: A fire spread through a village in MalaysiaвҖҷs Sabah state, destroying roughly 1,000 homes and displacing thousands.


In 2018, as you can see in this video that re-emerged over the weekend and went viral online, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer blamed Palestinians for there being вҖңno peace in the Middle EastвҖқ and invoked the Torah.

In 2026, Schumer continues to refuse to hold Israel accountable for war crimes and acts of ethnic cleansing, being one of seven Democrats to vote last week against blocking weapons to Israel. As Mehdi argued in FridayвҖҷs вҖҳFirst Draft,вҖҷ Senate Democrats need to do the right thing вҖ“ and replace him immediately.

Abdul El-SayedвҖҷs support is up 8 points in the latest Emerson poll, and he is now tied with Mallory McMorrow. I guess campaigning with Hasan Piker really hurt him, right? RIGHT??

Mike Waltz told ABCвҖҷs вҖҳThis WeekвҖҷ that вҖңall options are on the tableвҖқ in Iran, including destroying power plants and bridges, and rejected the idea that attacks on civilian infrastructure are war crimes.

The GOP is completely allergic to facts. When CNNвҖҷs Jake Tapper pressed Rep. Elise Stefanik on TrumpвҖҷs call to wipe out the вҖңwhole civilizationвҖқ of Iran, Stefanik refused to call the comments genocidal language. вҖңPresident Trump didnвҖҷt call for genocide, Jake. You are putting those words in his mouth,вҖқ she said.

The administration is seemingly escalating its efforts to claim the 2020 elections were rigged. FBI Director Kash Patel, a long-time election denier, told FoxвҖҷs Maria Bartiromo he has вҖңall the informationвҖқ he needs to make arrests soon.

Energy Secretary Chris Wright walked back claims that inflated gas prices would only last for вҖңweeks,вҖқ not вҖңmonths,вҖқ telling CNN he has no idea when prices will go below $3. вҖңThat could happen later this year,вҖқ Wright said. вҖңThat might happen until next year. But prices have likely peaked.вҖқ

The solution to gerrymandering is AI вҖ“ or so GOP Rep. Nicole Maliotakis proposed on CNNвҖҷs вҖҳState of the UnionвҖҷ as a way to produce fair districts, ahead of the Virginia redistricting referendum.

Monday, April 20: Elon Musk is set to be questioned by French cybercrime prosecutors in their ongoing investigation of X. Plus, UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer is expected to address the House of Commons regarding his appointment of Epstein-affiliate Peter Mandelson as ambassador to the US. And the Supreme Court is scheduled to hear oral arguments in Sripetch v. SEC, which could strip the SEC of its regulatory powers.

Tuesday, April 21: Virginians are set to vote on a new electoral map in which Democrats could win up to 10 House seats. Plus, US judges will hear arguments over the Justice DepartmentвҖҷs efforts to force New Mexico to hand over voter data.

Wednesday, April 22: California gubernatorial candidates Chad Bianco, Katie Porter, Tom Steyer, and Steve Hilton will participate in a debate on NewsNation.

Saturday, April 25: Trump is set to headline вҖңthe most exclusive crypto & business conferenceвҖқ at Mar-a-Lago for the top 297 holders of his $TRUMP crypto memecoin. HeвҖҷs also expected to attend the White House CorrespondentsвҖҷ Dinner for the first time as president.

ZeteoвҖҷs Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: 9 People Trump Once LOVED But Now Pretends He Always Hated
item.description: ItвҖҷs no secret that large-ego Donald Trump loves those who love him. вҖңWhen somebodyвҖҷs nice to me, I love that person,вҖқ he said earlier this month. вҖңEven if theyвҖҷre bad people.вҖқ Over the years, he has made that abundantly clear with his unwavering support of people who praise and applaud himвҖҰ untilвҖҰ
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
ItвҖҷs no secret that large-ego Donald Trump loves those who love him. вҖңWhen somebodyвҖҷs nice to me, I love that person,вҖқ he said earlier this month. вҖңEven if theyвҖҷre bad people.вҖқ

Over the years, he has made that abundantly clear with his unwavering support of people who praise and applaud himвҖҰ until they do otherwise, of course.

So, we compiled a list of 9 people Trump has previously praised for whom he has since revoked support.

Trump did not used to hide the fact that he and Jeffrey Epstein were the best of buddies. In 2002, when a New York magazine reporter asked Trump about Epstein, he responded: вҖңIвҖҷve known Jeff for 15 years. Terrific guy. HeвҖҷs a lot of fun to be with.вҖқ
The president went on to boast about their active social lives and acknowledged EpsteinвҖҷs taste for young women.

But, when asked about the disgraced sex offender last November, Trump had a very different response. вҖңI have nothing to do with Jeffrey Epstein,вҖқ he said. вҖңI threw him out of my club many years ago because I thought he was a sick pervert вҖ“ and I guess I turned out to be right.вҖқ Ironic that the change of attitude came a day before Congress approved the release of the Epstein files.

Tucker Carlson has been a long-time supporter of Trump and built his career on far-right commentary and debate. Trump has spoken highly of Carlson before, saying heвҖҷs a вҖңvery good person and a very good man.вҖқ ThatвҖҷs until CarlsonвҖҷs most recent disagreements with Trump on Israel and Iran which prompted a nasty snapback from Trump.

Since then, Trump has been on a rampage against Carlson on social media. On Friday, Trump took to Truth Social and put it simply, вҖңTucker is a Low IQ person.вҖқ He named Carlson alongside others on this list and even joked, вҖңI should do a list of good, bad, and somewhere in the middle. WouldnвҖҷt that be fascinating???вҖқ In another rant on Truth Social, Trump also refers to him as a вҖңHand flailing Fool.вҖқ

Unsurprisingly, Carlson was not the only person targeted by that post. Trump also mentioned Megyn Kelly, a conservative political commentator who heвҖҷs previously platformed at campaign speeches and referred to as a вҖңGREAT HONORвҖқ to have on stage.

Now though, Trump was sure to name drop her in with the list of вҖңNUT JOBS, TROUBLEMAKERS, [that] will say anything necessary for some вҖңfreeвҖқ and cheap publicity.вҖқ
This comes after Kelly called TrumpвҖҷs genocidal threats against Iran вҖңcompletely irresponsible and disgusting,вҖқ after the president threatened to kill IranвҖҷs вҖңwhole civilization.вҖқ

And that list goes on. Candace Owens, a far-right conspiracist and political commentator who Trump has previously recognized for her вҖңstar power,вҖқ has since publicly criticized Trump for his betrayal of MAGA.

So now, she is no longer a star but instead вҖңвҖҳCrazyвҖҷ Candace Owens, who accuses the Highly Respected First Lady of France of being a man.вҖқ And Trump didnвҖҷt stop there. He was also sure to comment on OwensвҖҷ appearance stating, вҖңthe First Lady of France is a far more beautiful woman than Candace, in fact, itвҖҷs not even close!вҖқ
Completely appropriate and professional behavior from the president.

The fourth person name-dropped in TrumpвҖҷs recent Truth Social post is Alex Jones. Trump once told the far-right radio host and conspiracist: вҖңYouвҖҷre amazing. I will not let you down.вҖқ

This obviously didnвҖҷt mean much, though. Now that Jones has criticized Trump on Iran, the president now refers to him now as вҖңBankrupt Alex Jones, who says some of the dumbest things.вҖқ He, of course, wraps up his list of name drops stating once and for all that вҖңThese so-called вҖҳpunditsвҖҷ are LOSERS, and they always will be!вҖқ

Classic.

Of course, we cannot make a list about people Trump has disowned and not mention former MAGA Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene. The politician who spent so long defending TrumpвҖҷs each and every move has been referred to by the president as a вҖңfantasticвҖқ personвҖқ and вҖңa very smart person.вҖқ

Toward the end of last year though, Greene began clashing with Trump over her support for releasing the Epstein files. Pretty soon, Trump turned her into вҖңMarjorie вҖҳTraitorвҖҷ Brown.вҖқ

Trump has repeatedly referred to her as such and made no secret of his feelings of betrayal. вҖңMarjorie вҖҳTraitorвҖҷ BrownвҖқ has yet to catch on with the broader public вҖ“ and MTG resigned rather than continue to run cover for Trump in Congress.

One of the most recent switch-ups from the president has been toward Riley Gaines. The Christian swimmer and anti-trans athlete has long been vocal in her support for Trump.

Trump last year said Gaines is вҖңa tremendous athlete, and it was a very unfair situationвҖҰ it was ridiculous, frankly,вҖқ referring to the time she tied for fifth place with a trans swimmer. As he signed an executive order targeting transgender athletes, Trump credited Gaines with being a вҖңleaderвҖқ of the anti-trans movement.

However, Gaines did not take well to TrumpвҖҷs recent depiction of himself as Jesus: вҖңWhy? Seriously, I cannot understand why heвҖҷd post this вҖҰ God shall not be mocked.вҖқ

This caused Trump to get defensive and revoke his support for Gaines: вҖңI didnвҖҷt listen to Riley Gaines. IвҖҷm not a big fan of Riley, actually.вҖқ

Funny how that keeps happening.

When Trump welcomed John Kelly as his chief of staff in 2017, he made clear that Kelly was an asset to the team.

In a post on X, then Twitter, he said Kelly was a вҖңGreat leaderвҖқ and вҖңa true star of my Administration.вҖқ

However, after Kelly resigned and publicly criticized Trump, the president was quick to turn to Truth Social and rave about how вҖңJohn Kelly is a LOWLIFE, and a bad General, whose advice in the White House I no longer sought, and told him to MOVE ON!вҖқ

Kelly did move on to be elected chair of the Marine Corps University FoundationвҖҷs board. Apparently, they thought Kelly was a good general.

And sometimes Trump even does the back-and-fourth within the same sentence.

When Joe Kent, former director of the National Counterterrorism Center, resigned over the Iran war around a month ago, Trump said he вҖңalwaysвҖқ thought Kent was вҖңa nice guyвҖқ but вҖңweak on security.вҖқ

вҖңItвҖҷs a good thing that heвҖҷs out,вҖқ Trump said.

And if there is one thing about Trump, he sure knows how to get inappropriate. He also dished on KentвҖҷs relationships, stating вҖңhe felt badly forвҖқ Kent вҖңsince he lost his wife,вҖқ Shannon M. Kent, who was killed by a bombing in 2019 while serving as a US Navy chief cryptologic technician in Syria. He complained that Kent вҖңremarried fairly quickly,вҖқ even though Kent did not marry again for four-and-a-half years.

Trump said, вҖңI told my people, вҖҳReach out to him, give him a job at the White House.вҖҷ This is the thanks I get.вҖқ

How considerate.

Though Trump isnвҖҷt feeling as generous nowadays and on Thursday, took to Truth Social to ask, вҖңWhoвҖҷs dumber, Tucker Carlson or Joe Kent?вҖқ Of course, he continued to ramble about KentвҖҷs wife and called him вҖңa SLEAZEBAG,вҖқ вҖңA LEAKER!вҖқ and вҖңa LOSER.вҖқ And just in case we missed any of the previous outbursts on social media he specified again: вҖңTucker, Candace, Megyn, and the rest of them are LOSERS.вҖқ

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: EXCLUSIVE: Documents Reveal US ContractorвҖҷs Modifications of Spy Plane Just Before Flights Over Gaza
item.description: UK surveillance flights over Gaza were conducted with more US involvement than previously known, Federal Aviation Administration documents obtained by Zeteo reveal. The near-daily spy flights, carried out from December 2023 through October 2025 and coordinated by the United KingdomвҖҷs Royal AirвҖҰ
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
UK surveillance flights over Gaza were conducted with more US involvement than previously known, Federal Aviation Administration documents obtained by Zeteo reveal.

The near-daily spy flights, carried out from December 2023 through October 2025 and coordinated by the United KingdomвҖҷs Royal Air Force using planes and equipment from US military contractors, put the countries at risk of aiding and abetting war crimes, international legal experts told Zeteo.

The UK maintains that intelligence gathered from the flights was solely for the purpose of locating hostages captured during the Oct. 7, 2023, attack by Hamas on Israel.

But lawmakers in the UK, UN officials, and human rights attorneys have expressed concerns that the intelligence could have been used by Israel for its military operations in Gaza, which have resulted in tens of thousands of civilian deaths and have been classified as genocide by both a United Nations investigative panel and a leading association of genocide scholars.

вҖңThe British and American governments would be on notice that intelligence they provided for targeting would likely be aiding and abetting war crimes, and to aid and abet a war crime is the same as to commit a war crime. ThatвҖҷs the potential criminal liability at stake here,вҖқ former director of Human Rights Watch Kenneth Roth told Zeteo.

On July 28, a pilot employed by the Nevada-based defense contractor Sierra Nevada Corporation seemingly forgot to disable a device that broadcasts flight details live during a surveillance flight over central Gaza, as first reported by Palestine Deep Dive (PDD).

The apparent mistake revealed that the plane, a highly modified Hawker Beechcraft Super King Air 350ER, circled for hours over central Gaza on the same day that multiple Israeli airstrikes killed at least 23 Palestinians in the same area, including women and children. The aircraft watched Gaza from above, using the callsign вҖңCROOK12.вҖқ Not long after, researchers with investigative outlet Declassified UK found another aircraft, owned by the same company, had also flown surveillance missions over Gaza months earlier.

The two aircraft are owned by Straight Flight Nevada Commercial Leasing LLC, a Nevada-based corporation, according to the FAA registration documents. The company is owned by Sierra Nevada Corporation, a large defense contractor that rarely makes headlines but has won US government defense contracts worth tens of billions of dollars.

Zeteo requested airworthiness documents from the Federal Aviation Administration on two aircraft and can reveal new details about the potential cause of the pilot error, as well as the role of US military contractors in a mission previously performed by the UKвҖҷs Royal Air Force.

Airworthiness documents are required by the FAA when US-registered aircraft are modified or overhauled.

According to the documents, the aircraft broadcasting CROOK12 had just days earlier been modified with highly controlled military equipment in the United States, including a device meant to identify вҖңfriendlyвҖқ or вҖңfoeвҖқ aircraft from other militaries, including the Israeli Air Force. This modification likely allowed the aircraft to be better integrated with existing Israeli military standards.

The documents, when paired with publicly available flight data, make it possible to track the modification, testing, and eventual export of the aircraft from a Sierra Nevada Corporation hangar in Hagerstown, Maryland, to the Royal Air Force Akrotiri base in Cyprus, where it would begin near daily surveillance flights in July 2025.

CROOK12 was modified with a communications device that allows military air traffic control systems to identify aircraft as friendly or potentially hostile, and to determine aeronautical information like speed and heading. The device is marked in the FAA paperwork as вҖңFor Military Use Only,вҖқ and requires special FAA permission to be tested.

The equipment, called an APX-119, is produced by RTX Corporation (formerly known as Raytheon Technologies) subsidiary Collins Aerospace and works with more than 100 types of vehicles and other systems used by the US Department of Defense, as well as multiple international users, per the company documentation. The system requires cryptographic input to protect against spoofing and misidentification, military aviation experts told Zeteo.

вҖңItвҖҷs a coded signal that can go from a ground station to an aircraft and determines whether that aircraft is a friend or foe,вҖқ Jim Cardoso, senior director for the University of South FloridaвҖҷs Global and National Security Institute, told Zeteo. вҖңIf youвҖҷre what we call вҖҳsquawking,вҖҷ you have a certain code in the [system], then the ground system knows that you are a friendly aircraft.вҖқ

Military contractors note that the APX-119 transponder has a special designation under the State DepartmentвҖҷs international trafficking in arms regulations (ITAR) regime. The equipment вҖңcounts as not only a defense article under ITAR jurisdiction, but is also Significant Military Equipment,вҖқ according to a training brochure from defense contractor PSI. This designation, reserved for equipment that is deemed вҖңwarranted because of their capacity for substantial military utility or capability,вҖқ according to federal regulations.

These devices are also used by the Israeli military. On Nov. 17, 2025, Department of Defense documents show that the US planned to transfer APX-119 units to Israel as part of an $18.82 billion arms deal that included new F-15IA fighter jets. The devices were mentioned in a joint resolution of disapproval of the arms transfer filed by Senator Bernie Sanders (I-VT).

The disclosure of the BeechcraftвҖҷs flight path over Gaza was due to what a UK military source told The Times last year was a вҖңschoolboy error.вҖқ While aviation analysts have speculated that this error could be related to pilotsвҖҷ use of the transponder, itвҖҷs unclear whether the APX-119 system was directly involved in the accidental disclosure.

According to the FAA documents, the aircraft had a civilian transponder system removed just before the APX-119 was added. The unit advertises that it can be used to broadcast civilian transponder signals as well as military identifiers.

The second aircraft, CROOK11, was outfitted with a Wescam MX-15, a powerful high-definition camera system built by L3 Harris, according to publicly available images of the aircraft. This system can capture heat and night vision imagery. Marketing materials advertise the system as вҖңAn Ideal Surveillance Solution for: ISR вҖ“ Medium-Altitude Covert Intelligence, Surveillance & Reconnaissance.вҖқ

That same aircraft was captured on an aviation enthusiastвҖҷs YouTube channel three years ago at an airshow in Hagerstown, Maryland, where Sierra Nevada Corporation operates a hangar. In the video, a placard displays the surveillance capabilities of the aircraft. Alongside the retractable MX-15 camera, the aircraft is outfitted with a jam-resistant GPS system. Israel has been known to jam GPS signals, creating safety issues for local commercial aviation.
The placard also lists вҖңprovisions for 7 different SI [signals intelligence] systems,вҖқ extended fuel tanks to allow for 7 hours of flight time, and provisions for вҖңmulti-level secure networks,вҖқ most likely for transmission of live data, in this case to the Israeli military.

The British surveillance flights began in December 2023 and initially used the RAF Shadow R1 aircraft, a Beechcraft King Air, similar to the Sierra Nevada Corporation aircraft, loaded with sensors, cameras, and radios. These flights continued almost daily until mid-2025, when the effort was mostly privatized and outsourced to the Sierra Nevada Corporation planes and pilots, according to publicly available flight data.

A report in the UKвҖҷs The Times, citing unnamed RAF officials, explained that the change was due to the existing Shadow fleet in need of maintenance and usage elsewhere.

вҖңThe safety of British nationals is our utmost priority. In support of the ongoing hostage rescue activity, the UK Ministry of Defence will conduct surveillance flights over the Eastern Mediterranean, including operating in airspace over Israel and Gaza,вҖқ Grant Schapps, the secretary of state for defense at the time, said in a statement in December 2023.

The last British hostage abducted during the October 7 attack to be held by Hamas in Gaza was released in January 2025. Yet the flights continued вҖ“ for months.

After the flight path was exposed in August, investigators like Iain Overton, executive director of UK nonprofit Action on Armed Violence, who had been chronicling the British militaryвҖҷs involvement in Gaza, took a closer look at Sierra Nevada CorporationвҖҷs aircraft. He found that the privatized mission had started earlier than UK military officials had previously disclosed.

Using historical public flight data, Overton found that another aircraft owned by Sierra Nevada Corporation had been broadcasting its location as it departed from Cyprus on Dec. 11, 2024, and circled over the Nuseirat refugee camp in central Gaza. The next day, Israel bombed the camp, killing more than 30 Palestinians and wounding over 50 others. Images and videos circulated online showing the bloody aftermath. вҖңThe [Israeli] occupation army knew that this is a residential block with many apartment buildings housing dozens of civilians, children, women and displaced people,вҖқ the Government Media Office in Gaza told Al Jazeera shortly after the attack. It is unclear whether the Israeli military used any intel that may have been provided from the flight the preceding day.

вҖңMinisters have repeatedly framed these missions as unarmed sorties focused on вҖҳhostage recovery,вҖҷ yet the scale and pattern of the flights suggest a broader ISR role. The aircraft have the capacity to map movement, intercept signals, and track activity across extensive areas,вҖқ Overton told Zeteo. вҖңThe aircraft involved were equipped with sensor suites capable of extraordinary precision. They can count the movements of armed actors, map the location of structures, and trace activity in real time.вҖқ

And yet Overton found that the British government, much like its US collaborator, wasnвҖҷt following its own policies on ensuring information shared with the Israelis wasnвҖҷt being used to kill civilians. He also encountered high levels of secrecy when he attempted to uncover information about the contract awarded to SNC.

вҖңThe Government insists that all foreign intelligence sharing must comply with the UKвҖҷs human-rights duties, but it refuses to set out the mechanisms by which this is guaranteed. When I requested the OSJA-linked intelligence risk-assessment forms that should accompany any sharing arrangement, the FCDO [UK equivalent to the Department of State] stated that it holds no such documents,вҖқ Overton said. вҖңKey details are withheld behind redactions, and the MoDвҖҷs refusal to discuss operational relationships with SNC or its subsidiaries means Parliament is effectively shut out.вҖқ

Overton characterized the lack of transparency as a вҖңdeliberate fog.вҖқ ItвҖҷs вҖңa system that relied on private firms to perform sensitive surveillance tasks while ensuring the public could not see what they do, what it costs, or how the information they collect was used.вҖқ

Several members of Parliament, tasked with providing oversight of the surveillance mission, have been stonewalled, met with claims that operational security outweighs the publicвҖҷs right to know.

вҖңIt is welcome that the UK surveillance flights over Gaza have stopped, but it does not remove the need for proper scrutiny,вҖқ UK Member of Parliament Helen Maguire, a Liberal Democrat, told Zeteo. вҖңThe government has still not sufficiently set out what outcomes those flights delivered in terms of supporting the recovery of the hostages, nor what safeguards were in place to ensure any intelligence gathered was not used in military operations by Israel.вҖқ

вҖңThe British public deserves clarity about the operations carried out in their name, and to know with certainty that the UK was not complicit in egregious human rights abuses in Gaza,вҖқ she added.

The Israeli embassy in Washington did not respond to ZeteoвҖҷs request, asking what procedures are in place to ensure the aerial intelligence was used solely for hostage rescue purposes.

Sierra Nevada Corporation, the company that owns and operates the surveillance aircraft, rarely makes headlines.

The company was the 27th largest Pentagon contractor last fiscal year, with over $2.07 billion in obligated defense dollars. Its top customers include the US Air Force and US Special Operations Command. Public job postings show the company is hiring pilots with active US вҖңSecretвҖқ security clearances for positions in Intelligence, Surveillance & Reconnaissance (ISR) missions, deployed in positions outside the continental US.

Zeteo reached out to SNC for comment, and to ask if its pilots active over Gaza are US citizens. The company declined to respond.

International legal and humanitarian experts told Zeteo that US contractors doing surveillance over Gaza could be held liable if they were providing information used in targeting for attacks carried out by Israel, due to the indiscriminate nature of IsraelвҖҷs bombardment.

While the death toll in Gaza is more than 72,340 people вҖ“ more than half of them women and children вҖ“ other estimates place the number significantly higher.

If information from the surveillance flights was shared with Israel and used for targeting, the lack of concern for civilians is the reason for legal liability, Kenneth Roth told Zeteo.

вҖңSome of the bombing was openly indiscriminate, such as dropping 2,000-pound bombs on neighborhoods. Were the British and American governments providing information about which neighborhood to bomb? [...] More recently, the big problem has been that even if the Israeli military was going after a presumed Hamas fighter, if they were tolerating up to 20 civilian deaths as an appropriate price to pay for killing one Hamas fighter, nobody in their right mind thinks that is a proportionate civilian cost.вҖқ

The UK Ministry of Defense said in a statement in October 2025 that it вҖңstrictly controlled what information was passed on and only information relating to hostage rescue was passed to the relevant Israeli authorities.вҖқ

The ministry added that вҖңthe surveillance aircraft were always unarmed, did not have a combat role, and were tasked solely to locate hostages.вҖқ

However, a UN report published just days after the ministryвҖҷs statement appeared to contradict the UKвҖҷs official narrative. The report noted the coincidence of the flights with Israeli operations.

вҖңFrom its bases in Cyprus, the UK has enabled a crucial US supply line to Tel Aviv and flown over 600 surveillance missions over Gaza throughout the genocide, sharing intelligence with Israel,вҖқ the UN report, authored by Francesca Albanese, United Nations special rapporteur on the situation of human rights in the Palestinian territory, found. вҖңFlight numbers and durations, often coinciding with major Israeli operations, suggest detailed knowledge and cooperation in the destruction of Gaza, extending beyond вҖҳhostage rescue.вҖҷвҖқ

A timeline of the surveillance missions

December 2023: RAF missions over Gaza begin using Shadow R1s, per Palestine Deep Dive (PDD). This is confirmed by Watkins/ The Times.

December 2023 through July 2025: 600 RAF Shadow Fleet flights over Gaza, per Watkins/Times.

Dec. 3, 2023, through March 27, 2025: Minimum of 518 ISR flights over Occupied Palestinian Territories, per Action on Armed Violence.

Oct. 10-15, 2024: N60125 [CROOK11] is вҖңrecommissionedвҖқ and modification of MANGA F system for GPS jamming protection, according to airworthiness documents.

Dec. 5, 2024: вҖңBeechcraft belonging to the US contractor with the registration N60125 arrived at Akrotiri on 5 December 2024, having flown from Hagerstown, Maryland, via St JohnвҖҷs, Reykjavik and Palermo,вҖқ per Declassified UK.

Dec. 11, 2024: Per Declassified UK: вҖңCROOK11 left Akrotiri at 20:14 UTC, arriving over Gaza at 21:10 UTC. It orbited above Nuseirat for around 20 minutes, its fixed track consistent with detailed optical or signals surveillance, before heading back to Cyprus, switching off its transponder en route at 22:17 UTC, and landing shortly after.вҖқ

Dec. 12, 2024: Israel bombs Nuseirat Camp, per Al Jazeera. Beechcraft Super King Air 350 with the registration N60125 and callsign CROOK11 circled over Gaza the same day, per Declassified UK.

Dec. 12, 2024: вҖң[T]he RAFвҖҷs Shadow ZZ419 left Akrotiri at 15:05 UTC, went dark near Gaza, and reappeared less than an hour before the bombing,вҖқ per Declassified UK.

July 10, 2025: US plane test mission from Hagerstown Regional Airport circling over Everett, Pennsylvania, per PDD. This plane is registered N6147U, per Times.

July 12, 2025: US plane flies to St. JohnвҖҷs regional airport in Newfoundland, per PDD.

July 13, 2025: US plane flies to Keflavik airport in Iceland, per PDD.

July 14, 2025: US plane flies to Belfast, then to Palermo, per PDD.

July 15, 2025: US plane arrives at RAF Akrotiri in Cyprus, per PDD.

July 18, 2025: US plane flies test circles off Akrotiri, per PDD.

July 20 to July 24, 2025: Daily flights over Gaza from 2pm to 8pm, escorted by RAF Shadow 1, per PDD. This is confirmed by Steffan Watkins for The Times.

July 25, 2025: Last RAF flight over Gaza before outsourcing to SNC, per PDD.

July 28, 2025: US plane forgets to turn off transponder after taking off at 8pm, per PDD. US plane arrives over Gaza at 9pm and circles for 90 minutes over Khan Younis. This is confirmed by The Times.

Aug. 4, 2025: Palestine Deep Dive report published.

Aug. 6, 2025: UK Times confirms US plane flights and UK hiring of SNC.

Aug. 12, 2025: Declassified UK report published.

Jason Paladino is an independent investigative reporter. Have a tip youвҖҷd like like to share? Contact Jason on Signal at jpal.01.

Tobias Burns is a reporter based in New York City. You can contact him on Signal at reporter.26

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: This Week in Palestine: Israeli Settlers Block Palestinian Children From School, and Soldiers Suspected of Rape CaвҖҰ
item.description: It was another deadly and violent week in Palestine. Israeli forces killed many, including children, across Gaza, Israeli settlers blocked Palestinian children from going to school and torched Palestinian cars, and the Israeli soldiers suspected of raping a Palestinian detainee could soon return toвҖҰ
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
It was another deadly and violent week in Palestine. Israeli forces killed many, including children, across Gaza, Israeli settlers blocked Palestinian children from going to school and torched Palestinian cars, and the Israeli soldiers suspected of raping a Palestinian detainee could soon return to reserve service.

There was some promising news, however: While the US Senate rejected measures to block arms and bulldozer sales to Israel, a record number of Senate Democrats voted for the legislation, reflecting increasing frustration with IsraelвҖҷs actions in Palestine, Lebanon, and across the region.

A quick note before we jump into todayвҖҷs вҖҳThis Week in PalestineвҖҷ: For now, weвҖҷre sending this newsletter to all Zeteo subscribers, but that wonвҖҷt always be the case. To ensure you get the newsletter every week, go to вҖҳmanage subscriptionвҖҷ in your Substack account, scroll down, and toggle on the button for вҖҳThis Week in PalestineвҖҷ. And please make sure youвҖҷre a paid Zeteo subscriber so you can read past the paywall.

Now, hereвҖҷs another troubling week in Palestine:

Police arrested more than 500 people, including elderly protesters and demonstrators with disabilities, in London at the first protest supporting Palestine Action since the UKвҖҷs High Court ruled that the groupвҖҷs ban was unlawful in February.

After the High CourtвҖҷs ruling, police initially had said they would stop arresting protesters at the Palestine Action demonstrations, but that changed after the UK government was approved to appeal the courtвҖҷs decision against the ban designating the group as a terrorist organization.

Activists, humanitarians, and hundreds of others officially launched a new flotilla aimed at breaking IsraelвҖҷs siege of Gaza. The flotilla, which is ultimately expected to include more than 100 boats and over 1,000 participants, is the latest attempt to make it to Gaza during the genocide. Previous fleets were violently intercepted by Israel in international waters.

Zeteo is aboard the flotilla and will be bringing you updates along the way.

Another former Gaza aid site security contractor has shed light on IsraelвҖҷs use of deadly force on unarmed aid seekers.

David McIntosh told Middle East Eye and Drop Site that he witnessed an Israeli sniper shoot a child who was playing at the site after receiving aid at a site run by the Gaza Humanitarian Foundation. вҖңHe was doing nothing but laughing and joking and they killed him,вҖқ McIntosh said. вҖңThereвҖҷs no way he survived.вҖқ

Israel killed at least 11 Palestinians, including a 3-year-old boy and a 14-year-old child, in strikes across Gaza вҖ“ the latest Israeli violations of the so-called вҖңceasefire.вҖқ

вҖңWhat is his fault? What is his crime? He should be wearing a wedding suit at his cousinвҖҷs today, but instead, he wore a shroud stained with blood,вҖқ a cousin of 14-year-old Adam Ahmed Halaa told Reuters after the boy was killed in a targeted strike on a police vehicle as the family walked to attend a wedding.

Israel has killed more than 72,340 Palestinians since the genocide began more than two years ago. That includes more than 760 killed since the вҖңceasefireвҖқ agreement was supposed to take effect last October.

Jailed popular Palestinian leader Marwan Barghouti, who was abducted and imprisoned by Israel 24 years ago, faces вҖңimmediate riskвҖқ due to a вҖңclear pattern of escalating abuse,вҖқ including violence, medical neglect, and treatment, while in Israeli prison, his lawyer said in a statement.

BarghoutiвҖҷs lawyer, Ben Marmarelli, said his client has been violently assaulted at least three times in as many weeks, including when Israeli prison guards unleashed a dog to repeatedly attack him. Barghouti remains extremely popular among Palestinians and Palestine supporters worldwide.

Learn more about Barghouti:

Israel is allowing five soldiers suspected of raping a Palestinian detainee to return to reserve duty, Haaretz reported.

The Israeli military dropped the abuse charges against the soldiers last month, claiming, among other things, that it didnвҖҷt have sufficient evidence because the Palestinian victim had been released back to Gaza, despite leaked video evidence and a doctorвҖҷs report that found the man arrived at the hospital with вҖңa ruptured bowel, a severe injury to his anus, lung damage and broken ribs.вҖқ

Israeli settlers torched multiple vehicles in a Palestinian village outside of Hebron, local news reported. Separately, settlers reportedly raided the homes of displaced Bedouins and chased livestock herders.

Earlier this week, Reuters reported that settlers had erected barbed wire to block dozens of Palestinian children from getting to their school since Monday. Several children reportedly experienced breathing difficulties after Israeli soldiers fired teargas in the area.

Miss last weekвҖҷs edition? Catch up here. Plus, check out more recent essays and interviews from Zeteo:

item.title: ThereвҖҷs No Such Thing As a вҖңDefensive WeaponвҖқ
item.description: Progressive presidential contender Congresswoman Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez made headlines earlier this month when she pledged to oppose funding any weapons for Israel, including вҖңdefensive capabilities,вҖқ a call soon echoed by fellow progressives including Senator Bernie Sanders . On Wednesday, afterвҖҰ
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Progressive presidential contender Congresswoman Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez made headlines earlier this month when she pledged to oppose funding any weapons for Israel, including вҖңdefensive capabilities,вҖқ a call soon echoed by fellow progressives including Senator Bernie Sanders. On Wednesday, after Senator Elissa Slotkin joined other possible centrist presidential hopefuls in casting their first-ever votes to block weapons sales to Israel, she reassured pro-Israel constituents by promising she would вҖңcontinue to support sending Israel much-needed defensive weapons like Iron Dome.вҖқ

So What Exactly Is a вҖңDefensive Weapon?вҖқ

To supporters of arming Israel, itвҖҷs an invaluable turn of phrase that implies a transferred weapon will only be used for the legally and morally unobjectionable purpose of self-defense. The вҖңdefensive weaponsвҖқ in question are a series of Israeli air defense/anti-air systems jointly funded and developed by the US and Israel. Politicians often refer to these colloquially as the вҖңIron Dome,вҖқ the name of one of the systems. Proponents of US funding for these systems argue they are purely defensive in nature and are needed to protect civilians and save lives. Who could object to that?

US military doctrine, for one. In joint military doctrine, thereвҖҷs no such thing as a вҖңpurely defensiveвҖқ weapon. ThatвҖҷs because any weapon can be used in support of offensive or defensive operations. Even air defense weapons, though they confusingly have вҖңdefenseвҖқ in the name. But thereвҖҷs nothing inherently offensive or defensive about any given system. In other words, per US military manuals, itвҖҷs not what you have, itвҖҷs how you use it.

The military does recognize protective capabilitiesвҖ”the weapons and equipment that help keep people alive during their mission. We can cut through the euphemisms hugging вҖңdefensiveвҖқ weaponry if we understand that protective doesnвҖҷt mean defensive.

Think about the helmet and pads on an offensive tackle in the NFL. Certainly protective for him, but not defensive for anyone, least of all the players heвҖҷs knocking over. Or think of the gas masks border patrol agents put on before throwing tear gas at protestors in Minneapolis. Or the armor on Israeli tanks shooting at tents in Gaza.

Even Netanyahu and Trump Know Air Defenses ArenвҖҷt Purely Defensive

If you still donвҖҷt believe anti-air weapons fit into this protective-not-defensive category, take it up with bleeding-heart peaceniks like вҖҰ Benjamin Netanyahu and Donald Trump.

As Reason editor Matthew Petti has pointed out, Netanyahu vociferously opposed RussiaвҖҷs sale of its long-range S-300 air defense system to Iran in 2015, with the Times of Israel reporting Netanyahu claimed вҖңthe deal would threaten the very survival of Israel.вҖқ

The Obama administration also registered its displeasure at the time, with Obama issuing an extra warning to Iran not to transfer these weapons to YemenвҖҷs Houthi rebels. Flash forward to earlier this week, when Trump threatened China with вҖңbig problemsвҖқ if it supplied Iran with new anti-air weapons. ThatвҖҷs a lot of stress for weapons that purportedly do nothing but save innocent lives.

Nobody can attest to the cruel irony of the term вҖңdefensive weaponsвҖқ better than the victims of IsraelвҖҷs recent military campaigns. The more вҖңdefensive weaponsвҖқ available, the bigger the offensive operations launched by the Israeli military and the higher the body count.

In response to escalating tit-for-tat attacks between Iran and Israel, Biden made the unprecedented decision to deploy a 100-soldier THAAD air defense battery to Israeli soil in October 2024. Days after the US air defense unit arrived, the Israeli military launched what was at the time its largest bombing run on Iran.

Netanyahu didnвҖҷt attack Iran directly again until the вҖң12-Day WarвҖқ in June 2025вҖ”incidentally just two months after Trump deployed a second THAAD to Israel.

And it beggars belief that Netanyahu would have urged Trump to join him in his latest battle against Iran without the confidence that layers of Israeli, US, and Gulf state air defenses would cushion the blow from inevitable Iranian retaliation.

ItвҖҷs hard to say these вҖңdefensive weaponsвҖқ protect civilian life when, without them, the thousands of civilians killed in Iran and Lebanon would probably still be alive today. Likewise, Israeli civilians, ostensibly the beneficiaries of these weapons, would be going about their daily lives as they were on February 27 instead of living in bomb shelters.

Notably, reports that the Israeli military is close to running out of air defense interceptors might explain why Netanyahu is grudgingly willing to go along with a ceasefire with Iran вҖ“ at least until he can reload.

If More Weapons Make IsraelвҖҷs Government More Aggressive, Does It Matter Who Pays for Them?

Calls to make Israel pay for its own weapons are mostly in reference to excluding Israel from a program called Foreign Military Financing. That program awards countries US taxpayer dollars, provided they use it to buy American weapons. Or, uniquely in IsraelвҖҷs case, spend it on their domestic defense industry.

However, pro-Palestinian advocacy group IMEU warns that demands to end military funding for Israel, but not arms sales altogether, are a Netanyahu-approved вҖңbait and switch.вҖқ The point of this strategy is to keep weapons flowing to Israel while mollifying the public by obfuscating continued US spending through joint weapons development schemes.

Additionally, selling вҖ“ instead of gifting вҖ“ weapons to Israel may circumvent Leahy Laws that prevent the US from transferring arms to human rights abusers, but donвҖҷt apply to commercial sales. Congress would have to take individual votes rejecting proposed sales, as the Senate did this week.

That Netanyahu and US Zionists are trying to weatherproof the US-Israel security relationship from outrage over Gaza should be considered an immense win for the anti-genocide, anti-war camp вҖ“ i.e. most Americans вҖ“ though it is certainly not a final victory.

And ending military financing for Israel would have some constraining effect on Israel. Without $21.7 billion in US subsidies for its post-Oct. 7 military operations from 2023 through 2025, the Israeli military may have chosen its targets more sparingly. For comparison, Israel spent $77 billion from its own treasury.

But Israel is still a wealthy enough country that can afford the weapons it needs to wreak havoc on the region. And anyway, the real value of US arms transfers isnвҖҷt that theyвҖҷre free, itвҖҷs that theyвҖҷre weapons Israel literally canвҖҷt get anywhere else.

Ultimately, the fundamental criticism of вҖңmake Israel pay for itвҖқ rings true: The moral, legal, and strategic logic of enabling IsraelвҖҷs genocide in Gaza, ethnic cleansing in the occupied West Bank and Lebanon, and war of aggression in Iran are the same whether the US bombs are sold or gifted.

Any debate on the costs of military support for Israel must also highlight the price of deploying the US military in support of Israel. Combat operations in the past six weeks have now dwarfed the costs of post-Oct. 7 military aid to Israel. But even before this war started, US military operations for the various post-Oct. 7 wars cost about half as much as military subsidies to Israel over the same period.

There is also a dearer price most politicians avoid discussing for fear of appearing insufficiently thankful to the troops: US casualties. But hundreds of military personnel have been wounded in the post-Oct. 7 wars, and at least 16 killed between the current Iran war and the 2024 attack on a US base in Jordan.

If you were wondering how the US troops have been wounded in Israel from the current war, they were probably from the air defense units Biden and Trump sent there.

But WouldnвҖҷt Israel Be Unsafe Without US Military Aid and Protection?

That was the argument put forth by the Biden officials who innovated the policy of unlimited, unconditional military support for Israel back in 2023 вҖ“ an argument former Secretary of State Antony Blinken is still making today.

But in 2026, we have ample evidence вҖ“ gathered at horrific cost вҖ“ that arms transfers, including вҖңdefensive weapons,вҖқ only give license to new wars that have done anything but protect civilians. Leaders and lobbies who truly want to bring an end to IsraelвҖҷs reign of terror will object to all forms of support for the Israeli military.

Harrison Mann is a former US Army major and executive officer of the Defense Intelligence AgencyвҖҷs Middle East/Africa Regional Center who resigned in protest of his officeвҖҷs support for IsraelвҖҷs war in Gaza under the Biden administration. He is currently with the group Win Without War and recently became a Zeteo contributor.

DonвҖҷt miss these other stories from Zeteo:
